Name: spectral_latency_optimization
Title: Spectral Latency Optimization: Training-Free Test-Time Optimization via PAC-Bayes Frameworks
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that spectral stability bounds and PAC-Bayes frameworks can be used to derive enforceable, strictly bounded-overhead protocols for latency-critical inference, enabling training-free test-time optimization and improving the robustness of language models to distributional shifts and noisy contexts.
Related Work: Our proposal builds upon recent works such as Spectral Quantization Routing, Training-Free Geometric Interference Cancellation, and Geometric Signal Separation, which have demonstrated the effectiveness of spectral methods and PAC-Bayes frameworks in optimizing latency and improving robustness in language models. However, our proposal distinguishes itself by focusing on training-free test-time optimization and providing a more comprehensive framework for spectral latency optimization.
Abstract: We propose a novel framework for spectral latency optimization, which leverages PAC-Bayes frameworks and spectral stability bounds to derive enforceable, strictly bounded-overhead protocols for latency-critical inference. Our framework enables training-free test-time optimization, improving the robustness of language models to distributional shifts and noisy contexts. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework through experiments on various benchmarks, showcasing its ability to achieve a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier while enforcing strict wall-clock latency targets.
Experiments: We will conduct experiments on various benchmarks, including WILDS and ICL benchmarks, to evaluate the effectiveness of our framework in optimizing latency and improving robustness. We will measure metrics such as accuracy retention, subspace alignment stability, and inference efficiency, and compare our results to state-of-the-art baselines. We will also provide a detailed analysis of the computational overhead and latency guarantees of our framework.
Risk Factors and Limitations: One potential risk factor is the computational overhead of our framework, which may be higher than that of existing methods. Additionally, our framework may not be effective in all scenarios, particularly those with high levels of noise or distributional shifts. However, we believe that our framework provides a significant improvement over existing methods and offers a promising direction for future research.
